content.text: The number of examples in a few-shot prompt — the "shot count" — has a non-linear effect on performance. Going from zero to one example often produces the largest single improvement because even one example anchors format and eliminates the most common misinterpretations. Going from one to three examples adds coverage of label boundaries and edge cases. Beyond five to eight examples, improvements on standard tasks typically plateau, and the cost (input tokens, latency, context budget) grows linearly. The optimal shot count is task-specific and should be found empirically on a held-out sample rather than assumed.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

EXAMPLE_POOL = [
    ("Absolutely love this product!",                    "Positive"),
    ("Terrible — broke after one use.",                  "Negative"),
    ("It works. Nothing special.",                       "Neutral"),
    ("Fast shipping, great quality.",                    "Positive"),
    ("Customer support was useless.",                    "Negative"),
    ("Packaging was damaged but product is fine.",       "Neutral"),
]

TEST_SET = [
    ("Exceeded all my expectations.",                    "Positive"),
    ("Complete waste of money.",                         "Negative"),
    ("Average product at an average price.",             "Neutral"),
    ("Arrived quickly and works perfectly.",             "Positive"),
    ("Instructions are confusing but it works.",        "Neutral"),
]


def classify(n_shots: int, query: str) -> str:
    shots = "\n\n".join(
        f"Text: {t}\nLabel: {l}" for t, l in EXAMPLE_POOL[:n_shots]
    )
    prefix = shots + "\n\n" if shots else ""
    prompt = f"{prefix}Text: {query}\nLabel:"
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=10,
        temperature=0.0,
        stop_sequences=["\n"],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text.strip()


print(f"{'N':>3}  {'Accuracy':>8}  {'Correct / Total'}")
for n in [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 6]:
    correct = sum(
        1 for text, gold in TEST_SET
        if classify(n, text).lower().startswith(gold.lower())
    )
    print(f"{n:>3}  {correct/len(TEST_SET):>8.0%}  {correct}/{len(TEST_SET)}")
Edge case coverage; consistency on diverse inputs
Modest gains over 3; meaningful for hard tasks
Consider fine-tuning instead at this scale

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def evaluate(n_shots: int, examples: list, test_set: list) -> float:
    """Return accuracy for a given shot count on a held-out test set."""
    correct = 0
    for text, gold_label in test_set:
        shots = "\n\n".join(
            f"Text: {t}\nLabel: {l}" for t, l in examples[:n_shots]
        )
        prefix = shots + "\n\n" if shots else ""
        prompt = f"{prefix}Text: {text}\nLabel:"
        resp = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
            max_tokens=10,
            temperature=0.0,
            stop_sequences=["\n"],
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        )
        pred = resp.content[0].text.strip()
        if pred.lower().startswith(gold_label.lower()):
            correct += 1
    return correct / len(test_set)


def find_optimal_n(
    examples: list,
    test_set: list,
    candidates: list[int] | None = None,
    min_accuracy: float = 0.90,
) -> int:
    """Return the smallest N that meets the accuracy threshold."""
    if candidates is None:
        candidates = [0, 1, 2, 3, 5]
    for n in sorted(candidates):
        acc = evaluate(n, examples, test_set)
        print(f"  N={n}: {acc:.0%}")
        if acc >= min_accuracy:
            return n
    return candidates[-1]  # return largest if threshold not met


# Example usage (small test_set for illustration)
EXAMPLES = [
    ("Great product!",              "Positive"),
    ("Terrible quality.",           "Negative"),
    ("Average, nothing special.",   "Neutral"),
    ("Very happy with purchase.",   "Positive"),
    ("Broken on arrival.",          "Negative"),
]
TEST = [
    ("Works as expected.",          "Neutral"),
    ("Would buy again!",            "Positive"),
    ("Stopped working in a week.",  "Negative"),
]

print("Searching for minimum N meeting 90% accuracy:")
best = find_optimal_n(EXAMPLES, TEST, min_accuracy=0.90)
print(f"Optimal N: {best}")

The rule of thumb is: start with 3 balanced examples and measure. If accuracy meets your threshold, stop there — adding more examples will not recover tokens spent, and the diminishing-return curve means the next few examples are unlikely to move the needle. If accuracy is still below threshold after 5–8 well-chosen examples, the problem is probably not shot count — it is exemplar quality, prompt framing, or a task that genuinely requires fine-tuning.

"Number of shots" in few-shot prompting refers to the count of demonstration examples included in the input prompt before the actual query. A zero-shot prompt provides no examples; a one-shot prompt provides one; a few-shot prompt typically provides two to eight. The shot count is the primary tunable parameter when using in-context learning with large language models, and it directly trades off prompt token cost against task performance.

In-context learning (ICL) is the mechanism by which LLMs learn from examples embedded in the prompt at inference time — without updating any model weights. The examples serve as implicit demonstrations of the input-output mapping the model should follow, including output format, reasoning style, and label vocabulary. The shot count determines how many such demonstrations are available to the model at prediction time.

For most practical NLP tasks, the number of shots is one of the highest-impact prompt-engineering decisions. Going from zero to one example can improve accuracy by 10–30 percentage points on classification tasks, simply by anchoring the model to the expected output format. Failing to include any examples is one of the most common reasons LLM outputs are misformatted or misclassified in production systems.

Beyond format anchoring, the shot count affects the model's calibration on the specific label set you care about. A zero-shot classifier might output "very positive" when you only want "positive" or "negative"; a single well-chosen example resolves this ambiguity immediately. More examples increase coverage of edge cases and help the model handle distribution shift in the query — but each additional example also costs input tokens and increases per-request latency and cost.
: Performance improvements from adding shots require no fine-tuning, no labelled training set (beyond the examples), and no infrastructure changes.
: Changing the shot count or swapping example content is as fast as editing a string — the feedback loop is seconds, not hours.
: Even one example demonstrably teaches output format (JSON, bullet points, single labels), reducing post-processing effort.
: Few-shot prompting is model-agnostic and portable — the same examples work across GPT, Claude, Gemini, and open-source models, with only minor wording adjustments.
: Each example adds ~30–200 tokens to the prompt. At 8 examples × 150 tokens, that is 1,200 extra tokens per request — a meaningful cost at scale.
: Many-shot prompts compete with the query for the model's finite context window. At high shot counts the query itself may need to be truncated.
Example quality matters more than quantity
: Poor examples can actively hurt performance by demonstrating wrong outputs or biasing the model toward incorrect label distributions. Three excellent examples beat eight mediocre ones.
: Beyond 5–8 shots, accuracy gains typically flatten. Adding shots 9–16 buys marginal improvement while doubling token cost — at that point fine-tuning is likely the better investment.
How It Works — The Mechanics of In-Context Learning

Each demonstration example adds a structured input-output pair to the context that the model's attention mechanism can attend to when generating its response. From a probabilistic perspective, the model is approximating the conditional:

attrs.latex: P(y \mid x, e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_N) \approx P(y \mid x)_{\text{fine-tuned on } \{e_i\}}

content.text: is the query. The examples act as implicit prior information that shifts the model's output distribution toward the demonstrated pattern without any weight updates.
The accuracy improvement from going from N to N+1 shots is approximately logarithmic in N. Empirically, the marginal gain from shot k can be modelled as:
is a task-dependent constant. This explains why the single biggest gain is always from 0 to 1 shot, and gains diminish rapidly thereafter.

attrs.alt: Graph showing accuracy vs number of shots: large jump from 0 to 1, diminishing returns after 3-5 shots, increasing token cost shown dashed

content.text: When to Use Which Shot Count
Simple, well-known tasks; tight context budget; latest large models
Custom output formats; niche label sets; tasks with many valid phrasings
Format anchoring; low token budget; a single representative example exists
Multi-class classification where one example can't cover all labels
Binary/ternary classification with balanced examples per class; standard default
Very long individual examples that would overflow context
Tasks with edge cases; multi-class classification; handling distribution variety
When accuracy plateau is already reached; when API cost is constrained
Fine-grained tasks with many classes; long-context models (128k+ context)
Standard tasks; consider fine-tuning instead at this scale

A customer support system routes incoming tickets to one of five teams: Billing, Technical, Returns, Account, and General. The following shows how to evaluate shot counts to find the minimum N that meets a 90% accuracy SLA on a representative sample:

import anthropic
import json
from typing import Optional

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

LABELS = ["Billing", "Technical", "Returns", "Account", "General"]

EXAMPLE_POOL = [
    ("My invoice shows double charges for last month.",        "Billing"),
    ("The app keeps crashing on iOS 17.",                      "Technical"),
    ("I want to return my order, it arrived damaged.",         "Returns"),
    ("I can't log in, please reset my password.",             "Account"),
    ("What are your business hours?",                          "General"),
    ("Why was my subscription renewed without notice?",        "Billing"),
    ("The API is returning 500 errors on POST requests.",     "Technical"),
    ("Return label for order #ORD-8821 not in my email.",    "Returns"),
]

TEST_TICKETS = [
    ("Charged twice this week, please refund.",                "Billing"),
    ("App won't open on my Android phone.",                   "Technical"),
    ("Item not as described, want to send it back.",          "Returns"),
    ("Need to update my email address on file.",              "Account"),
    ("Are you open on weekends?",                             "General"),
    ("Where is the developer documentation?",                 "Technical"),
]


def classify_ticket(text: str, examples: list, n: int) -> str:
    shots = []
    for inp, label in examples[:n]:
        shots.append({"role": "user", "content": f"Ticket: {inp}\nCategory:"})
        shots.append({"role": "assistant", "content": label})

    shots.append({"role": "user", "content": f"Ticket: {text}\nCategory:"})

    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=10,
        temperature=0.0,
        stop_sequences=["\n"],
        system=f"Classify support tickets into one of: {', '.join(LABELS)}. Reply with the category name only.",
        messages=shots,
    )
    return resp.content[0].text.strip()


print(f"{'N':>3}  {'Accuracy':>8}")
for n in [0, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8]:
    correct = sum(
        1 for text, gold in TEST_TICKETS
        if classify_ticket(text, EXAMPLE_POOL, n).lower() == gold.lower()
    )
    acc = correct / len(TEST_TICKETS)
    print(f"{n:>3}  {acc:>8.0%}  {'✓' if acc >= 0.90 else '✗'}")
: Using 3 positive examples and 1 negative example biases the model toward predicting positive. Always aim for roughly equal representation across all labels in your example pool.
: Examples that are obviously in one category don't teach the model about edge cases. Include at least one ambiguous or borderline example per relevant boundary.
: Adding shots without measuring the effect on a held-out sample is a waste of tokens. Always evaluate empirically before committing to a shot count.
Reusing examples from the test set
: Selecting examples that appear in your evaluation set inflates measured accuracy. Keep example pools and evaluation sets strictly separate.
: This covers the most common classes and provides format anchoring. Measure accuracy, then add more only if needed.
: Avoid picking several examples with identical phrasing or topics. Each example should cover a different sub-case or linguistic variation.
: Retrieve the k examples most similar to the incoming query from a pool using embedding similarity (kNN retrieval). This gives query-adaptive few-shot prompting without changing N.
Cache prompts when N is fixed
: If shot count and examples are static across requests, use prompt caching (Anthropic) or system message caching (OpenAI) to avoid re-tokenising and re-attending to the examples on every call.
The latency impact of increasing the shot count is proportional to the number of additional input tokens processed. For a model with

attention complexity (standard transformer), doubling the context length roughly quadruples the prefill compute — though in practice, batched KV-cache operations make the real-world overhead closer to linear for the example tokens.

At scale (millions of requests per day), the cost difference between 1-shot and 8-shot is approximately 7× the per-example token count in input cost. For a 100-token example at $3/M input tokens, this is $0.0003 per request —
extra per 100,000 requests. This is usually acceptable, but at 10M+ daily requests the cumulative cost warrants careful shot-count optimisation.
Cache static few-shot examples; pay only for cache read on repeated calls
Automatic few-shot example selection and optimisation via bootstrapped labelling
Dynamic example selection from example stores using semantic similarity
Evaluate accuracy vs. shot count as part of prompt evaluation pipelines

Few-shot examples embedded in prompts can be exfiltrated through prompt injection attacks — a user query designed to instruct the model to "repeat the examples above." If your examples contain sensitive data (customer PII, proprietary labelling heuristics), store them server-side and inject them via a protected system prompt rather than in the visible user-facing context. Rate-limit and monitor for unusually long outputs that might indicate example exfiltration.

For reliability, pin example content under version control and evaluate accuracy when examples are updated. A well-intentioned example change can inadvertently reduce accuracy on other cases. Treat your example pool as a mini test suite: run the accuracy evaluation script on every PR that touches the examples, just as you would run unit tests.

If accuracy does not improve beyond 5–8 shots, the bottleneck is example quality, prompt framing, or task complexity — not shot count.
